gist: In a pre-recorded weekly webinar, Ed Yardeni argues Q2 2026 earnings expectations (~21-22% YoY, tech at 65.5%) are so exuberant that upside surprises are unlikely, while new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh has quietly turned hawkish, set up five reform task forces, and is grappling with AI's short-term inflationary bite (chip, construction, and electricity prices) even as it may be creating jobs and lifting productivity.

insights.claim: Wall Street analysts are pricing in roughly 21.6% year-over-year S&P 500 earnings growth for Q2 2026, another 21.6% for Q3, and around 26% for Q4, expectations so elevated that Ed Yardeni thinks upside surprises this earnings season are very unlikely.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: With little cushion in estimates, in-line results could still trigger volatility; watch for sector rotation rather than a broad market top.
insights.quote: This time around it's very unlikely we're going to have any upside surprises given how strong the expectations are for the second quarter.

insights.claim: Historically, companies expecting weak results leak that to analysts ahead of earnings season (prompting downward estimate revisions), while companies expecting blockbuster results stay quiet to maximize the surprise, which is why estimates typically get cut into earnings season and then beaten.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: This cycle breaks the normal pattern because estimates were revised UP going into Q2, removing the usual setup for beats.

insights.claim: Even if Q2 results merely meet already-exuberant consensus estimates, the market could sell off because those expectations are already priced in — a dynamic Yardeni says was previewed by Samsung's stock reaction to its own results.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: In-line-but-not-better prints in high-expectation names (especially tech) are a sell-the-news risk, not just misses.
insights.quote: We saw that with Samsung by the way as a sort of a possibility of what could happen.

insights.claim: Any earnings-season pullback is likely to be a buying opportunity rather than a bear-market or correction trigger, and could accelerate rotation away from S&P 500 growth stocks — where earnings expectations are most inflated — into value.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Investors can lean into growth-to-value rotation trades around Q2/Q3 earnings rather than de-risking broadly.

insights.claim: Second-quarter 2026 earnings growth expectations vary sharply by sector: technology is expected up 65.5% year-over-year, materials up 32.6%, energy up 117% (oil-price dependent), and healthcare is the only sector expected to post a decline.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Technology carries by far the highest bar this season, raising the odds of a sell-the-news reaction even on strong prints.

insights.claim: New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh has created five three-person task forces to study Fed reform issues, including what economic data the Fed should track and how AI affects productivity and inflation, with results due by the end of 2026 — giving him cover to defer specifics during congressional testimony.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Expect Warsh to repeatedly punt specific policy-reform questions in this week's congressional testimony by citing pending task-force reports; markets won't get real Fed-structure clarity until year-end.

insights.claim: Despite widespread expectations that Kevin Warsh would run a dovish Fed based on his tone during the job interview process, he joined the FOMC's hawkish pivot in June and reiterated that price stability is the committee's top priority.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Repricing Warsh as a hawk rather than a dove argues against betting on near-term rate cuts under his leadership.
insights.quote: The only surprise back then was that even Kevin Worsh signed up and joined the consensus.

insights.claim: Bond vigilantes have stayed in a neutral holding pattern, but Treasury yields would likely be even higher if Kevin Warsh had not come across as hawkish, since that hawkish signaling has itself reassured markets the Fed remains inflation-focused.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A shift back toward dovish rhetoric from Warsh could be the trigger that finally provokes bond vigilantes into pushing yields higher.

insights.claim: Yardeni doubts Kevin Warsh will actually order regional Fed presidents to stop giving speeches and interviews, even though Warsh favors less Fed communication and wants markets to set rate expectations from data rather than Fed guidance — meaning the Fed's public chatter (which Yardeni calls the "Federal Open Mouth Committee") will likely continue.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Fed-speak volatility around individual regional president comments is likely to persist rather than get centrally controlled.
insights.quote: I've called them the Federal Open Mouth Committee.

insights.claim: The FOMC's June 2026 meeting minutes contained extensive discussion of AI's inflationary consequences but relatively little on its productivity-enhancing, disinflationary potential, reflecting a growing Fed consensus that AI is currently a near-term inflation risk.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Fed officials are more likely to cite AI as a reason for caution on rate cuts than as a disinflationary tailwind in the near term.

insights.claim: AI-driven demand is showing up directly in producer and consumer prices: PPI electronic components and accessories are up 26.9% year-over-year, CPI computer software and accessories are up 14.5%, PCE computers and peripherals are up 1.3%, and electricity prices are up 5.9%, which Yardeni links to data-center-related demand.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Component-level price data is an early tell for whether AI infrastructure spending leaks into broader consumer inflation.

insights.claim: Apple's recent decision to raise prices could be an early sign that AI/chip-driven component cost inflation, which hasn't visibly hit consumer electronics prices yet (PCE computers and peripherals only up 1.3%), is starting to pass through to end consumers.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Watch consumer electronics pricing from major OEMs as a leading indicator of AI-chip cost pass-through to consumers.

insights.claim: Job postings explicitly mentioning AI have risen to 5.7% of listings and are trending higher, while professional/business-services job openings and manufacturing employment (PMI) are showing early signs of turning up, which Yardeni reads as tentative evidence AI is currently a net job creator rather than destroyer.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: If job openings mentioning AI keep climbing, it supports a case for AI-driven labor-market resilience rather than the displacement narrative.

insights.claim: Construction employment is holding at a record high even though residential construction is weak, because data-center and broader manufacturing-facility construction activity is offsetting the residential drag.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Data-center capex is now a large enough employment driver to mask weakness in traditional housing construction — a trend to track for regional labor-market strength.

insights.claim: Rising U.S. business-formation applications may partly reflect laid-off tech workers regrouping with former colleagues to start their own companies, rather than pure job destruction from AI-driven layoffs.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Elevated business formations could understate the true entrepreneurial spillover benefit of AI-related tech layoffs.

themes.theme: A rising neutral real interest rate

insights.claim: The neutral real interest rate is likely rising because retiring baby boomers are drawing down savings (evidenced by a sharp increase in Social Security benefit payments), stricter immigration limits are reducing the number of foreign workers who would otherwise be saving, and heavy debt issuance is financing data-center capex — a combination pushing the personal savings rate down even as capital demand rises.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A structurally higher neutral rate argues against assuming Fed policy is as restrictive as the nominal fed funds rate suggests.

insights.claim: If the Fed fails to recognize that the neutral real interest rate has risen, its current policy stance risks being too accommodative, which Yardeni sees as more likely to fuel speculative asset bubbles than a sustained rise in consumer price inflation.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Frame Fed-easy-policy risk as an asset-bubble concern (equities, private credit, data-center debt) rather than solely a CPI concern.

insights.claim: "Supercore" services inflation (services excluding energy and housing) has ticked up to 3.9%, and Kevin Warsh's preferred trimmed-mean PCE measure sits at 2.41% — both still above the Fed's 2% target — even as unit labor cost inflation has fallen to just 0.5%, a divergence Yardeni reads as productivity gains offsetting wage pressure.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Falling unit labor costs alongside above-target supercore inflation supports the productivity/AI narrative rather than a wage-price spiral.

insights.claim: Yardeni's team prefers a CPI or PCE inflation measure that excludes owners'-equivalent rent entirely, calling the imputed-rent concept a "nonsensical" and "cockamamie" idea, and argues OER is a major reason headline inflation still reads above the Fed's 2% target.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Investors relying on headline CPI/PCE may be overstating true inflation pressure if OER is distorting the print.
insights.quote: It's a nonsensical concept. It's what would owners have to pay in rent to themselves if they were their own landlords. It's a really cockami idea.

insights.claim: The Magnificent 7 has rebounded recently but is still lagging year-to-date, up only 2.6% versus 14.3% for the rest of the S&P 500 (the "X-Mags"), while value stocks are hitting new highs — evidence of broadening market participation beyond mega-cap growth.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: The broadening-market trade (ex-Mag7, value, small/mid caps) has legs and isn't just a rotation blip.

insights.claim: Market concentration, measured by the S&P 100 relative to the S&P 500, has not reached the extremes seen during the late-1990s tech bubble, which Yardeni argues reduces the odds of a similarly violent concentration unwind.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Concentration-driven crash fears may be overstated relative to the dot-com era comparison many bears invoke.

insights.claim: Using a "PE times E" framework, Yardeni notes that if S&P 500 forward earnings hold near $400 through year-end and the market applies roughly a 20x multiple, the index has room to keep grinding higher into 2027 as forward earnings continue rising.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A simple earnings-times-multiple framework keeps Yardeni structurally bullish on the index absent a major multiple compression.

insights.claim: Consumer strength remains evident in resilient TSA airport-checkpoint travel volumes and solid retail sales even as airfares rise, reinforcing that the Fed's dual-mandate focus can shift more fully toward price stability rather than labor-market support.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Resilient consumer spending data removes one argument for near-term Fed rate cuts.

insights.claim: Baby boomers alone are sitting on roughly $89.8 trillion in net worth, and including the silent generation, senior citizens collectively hold on the order of $100 trillion — a wealth base Yardeni cites as structural support for consumer spending.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: This wealth cushion underpins Yardeni's ongoing bullish consumer-spending thesis even amid softer housing and immigration-driven labor shifts.

insights.claim: Because the Fed's 100-basis-point rate cut in 2024 and 75-basis-point cut in 2025 were framed as insurance against labor-market deterioration that hasn't materialized, Yardeni questions whether that easing was really necessary and suggests policy may now be too accommodative.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: If the Fed agrees its prior insurance cuts were unneeded, that strengthens the case for holding rates higher for longer rather than cutting further.

insights.claim: The US and Iran remain in an active shooting war, with US military strikes targeting Iranian positions near the Strait of Hormuz aimed at degrading Iran's ability to threaten commercial and oil-tanker transit through the strait.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Continued military action near the Strait of Hormuz is a live tail risk for oil-supply disruption and energy-price spikes.
